coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly, bright luminescent carbon dots and their potential applications for detecting hypochlorous acid in water and live cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Venkatesan, Parthiban
Govindasamy, Rajakumar
Periyasami, Govindasami
Rahaman, Mostafizur
Pandiaraj, Saravanan
Thiruvengadam, Muthu
Thirumalaivasan, Natesan
Wu, Shu-Pao
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we utilized Agaricaceae, or mushrooms, as the carbon source for the hydrothermal production of multifunctional carbon dots (MCDs). The MCDs showed remarkable features such as bright blue fluorescence, exceptional biocompatibility, high quantum yield, and efficient biolabeling ability. Moreover, the MCDs displayed extraordinary specificity towards HOCl, and could detect it in deionized water with a lower detection limit of 3.38 nM. Additionally, the MCDs had potent antioxidant properties, low cytotoxicity, and high cell permeability, as confirmed by DPPH and reducing potential analysis. Furthermore, the MCD probe proved to be highly useful in various biological applications, including the detection of endogenous HOCl in the RAW 264.7 cell line and the assessment of PMA-induced endogenous HOCl formation using confocal imaging. The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from a natural resource such as mushrooms is a promising prospect due to their exceptional physicochemical properties and broad range of potential applications.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology:2022
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hypochlorous acid

Carbon dot
Biocompatibility
Antioxidant
Fluorescent character
Cell imaging

This study was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (Taiwan) under a grant ( MOST 111-2113-M-A49-001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of MCDs and the inset shows enlarged TEM image of carbon dot. (b) Particle size distribution of MCDs. (c) The XRD patterns of MCDs. (d) FTIR spectra of MCDs.
Selectivity of (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (b) fluorescence spectral chances of MCDS luminescence in the presence of various anions (15 μM).

Maximum intensity of 441 nm fluorescence exhibited by MCDs treated with 15 μM of various anion species in H2O. MCDs with no additional anions are represented by the black bar, MCDs with other anions are represented by the blue bar, and MCDs with HOCl are represented by the red bar, all at 15 μM. The excitation wavelength was 370 nm.
a) UV–Vis spectra of MCDs with the addition of various concentrations of HOCl (0–15 μM). (b) MCDs fluorescence spectra at different HOCl concentrations (0–15 μM) in water solution.

The antioxidant activity of MCDs was investigated using the Reducing power method (positive control (10–100 μg/mL) BHT) and MCDs (10–100 μg/mL), (A) DPPH method, (B) Reducing power (The data were presented with error bars as the mean ± SD of three measurements).
Comparing the vitality of RAW264.7 cells exposed to varying concentrations of MCDs, with DMSO acting as a positive control (n = 3).
Detection of PMA-induced HOCl production in RAW264.7 cells. (a) The cells treated with MCDs for 30 min at 37 °C. (b) PMA (25 ng/mL) for 2 h at 37 °C in the presence of MCDs.
Preparation process of MCDs by hydrothermal method from mushroom for HOCl sensing and biological (antioxidant and live cell imaging) applications.
A comparison of various probes' HOCl detection abilities.

row.entry: in vitro level detection of HOCl in 4T1 cells imaging.
in vitro level detection of HOCl in Hela, MCF-7 and H1299 cells imaging.
in vitro level detection of HOCl in MCF-7 cells imaging.
Detection of HOCl in water samples.
in vitro level detection of HOCl in SMMC7721 cells imaging.
Detection of HOCl in tap water samples.
in vivo level detection of HOCl in A549 cells imaging and zebrafish.
in vivo level detection of HOCl in RAW264.7 cells imaging.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly, bright luminescent carbon dots and their potential applications for detecting hypochlorous acid in water and live cell imaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have made equal contributions to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we utilized Agaricaceae, or mushrooms, as the carbon source for the hydrothermal production of multifunctional carbon dots (MCDs). The MCDs showed remarkable features such as bright blue fluorescence, exceptional biocompatibility, high quantum yield, and efficient biolabeling ability. Moreover, the MCDs displayed extraordinary specificity towards HOCl, and could detect it in deionized water with a lower detection limit of 3.38 nM. Additionally, the MCDs had potent antioxidant properties, low cytotoxicity, and high cell permeability, as confirmed by DPPH and reducing potential analysis. Furthermore, the MCD probe proved to be highly useful in various biological applications, including the detection of endogenous HOCl in the RAW 264.7 cell line and the assessment of PMA-induced endogenous HOCl formation using confocal imaging. The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from a natural resource such as mushrooms is a promising prospect due to their exceptional physicochemical properties and broad range of potential applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology is a rapidly developing area of research with the potential for a wide range of applications due to the unique characteristics of materials at the nanoscale [1–3]. In particular, carbon dots (CDs), which measure less than 10 nm, have been gaining traction due to their beneficial properties, such as low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, high aqueous solubility, and biocompatibility [4–7]. Various techniques have been used to synthesize CDs, including acidic oxidation [8], arc discharge [9], electrochemical oxidation [10], ultrasonic [11], microwave [12], supported synthetic [13], hydrothermal [14], and laser ablation [15]. While these methods are effective, they are often costly and inefficient, requiring tedious purification procedures [16–28] (see Scheme 1 ).

In recent years, great strides have been made to create an environmentally friendly approach for producing carbon dots from inexpensive precursors, such as orange peel, mint, Phyllanthus emblica, and garlic [29–32]. However, the previously reported CDs have limited photostability when in a complex environment, which restricts their application in biomedicine [33–38]. To maximize their potential, a greater understanding of their essential features is essential. The Agaricaceae family of basidiomycete fungi, commonly known as mushrooms, have been utilized for their medicinal benefits for many years [39]. They contain numerous chemical compounds, such as phenolic compounds, indole compounds, and carotenoids, and are rich in vitamins and minerals with antioxidant properties [40,41]. Researchers have begun to explore the potential of mushrooms in various fields [42,43]. This study aims to develop a biocompatible luminescent carbon dot that is both selective and sensitive to overcome the limitations of intracellular HOCl detection methods.

The past decade has seen significant advances in the production of carbon dots (CDs) using cost-effective materials through eco-friendly techniques. For instance, fluorescent CDs can be generated from natural materials such as orange peel, mint, Phyllanthus emblica, and garlic, either in organic solvents or with a lower quantum yield (QY) [44,45]. While some of the previously produced CDs have displayed low photostability in complex environments, such as varying pH levels, high ionic strength, or intense light exposure [46], understanding their essential features is crucial to maximize their potential applications, particularly in biomedicine.

Hypochlorous acid (HOCl) has become increasingly popular for its practical applications, such as water disinfection, bleaching, and cyanide treatment [47]. Its strong oxidizing properties make it valuable in various biological conditions [48]. In vivo, HOCl is produced as a result of the reaction between chloride and hydrogen peroxide in phagocytes, catalyzed by the enzyme myeloperoxidase, leading to its antimicrobial effects in neutrophils [49]. HOCl can oxidize different biomolecules, such as DNA, RNA, fatty acids, proteins, and cholesterol, and its unchecked production in humans has been associated with various diseases and conditions, such as tissue damage, heart disease, neurodegeneration, cancer, rheumatoid arthritis, and Parkinson's disease [50–52]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a detection technique that is simple, selective, and sensitive in biological systems to identify the presence of HOCl.

In this study, we have developed a cost-effective and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method for synthesizing mushroom-based carbon dots (MCDs) that circumvents the need for expensive chemicals, high temperatures, and strong acids previously used in other methods. These MCDs were utilized to detect low levels of HOCl ions in natural water, offering a simpler, more effective, and more reliable approach than the previously recommended commercial fluorescence method. The MCDs exhibited excellent selectivity for HOCl, with a detection limit of 3.38 nM, surpassing the sensitivity of several previous methods. Our results suggest that these biocompatible MCDs could be used for fast and accurate detection of HOCl ions in RAW 264.7 cells, while also demonstrating strong antioxidative properties.

To prepare for future research, mushrooms were obtained from a nearby grocery store and rinsed with deionized water. No further purification of the laboratory-grade chemicals was performed before their use. All experimental water was double-distilled. Sigma-Aldrich was used to purchase the penicillin-streptomycin and 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT). An Agilent 8453 spectrometer was employed to record the UV–Vis absorbance bands. A Hitachi F-4500 spectrometer was used to acquire the fluorescence measurements. To investigate the chemical interactions in the C-dots, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed using KBr pellets within the wavenumber range of 650 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The cellular imaging studies were conducted with a Leica TCS-SP5-X AOBS confocal camera manufactured in Germany. TEM images were captured using a 120 kV Hitachi High-tech HT7700.

The hydrothermal synthesis method outlined in Fig. 1 was used to produce luminescent carbon dots (MCDs) from mushroom in this study. The process involved suspending 1 g of mushrooms in 20 mL of deionized water and subjecting the mixture to elevated temperatures of 200 °C for 5 h in an autoclave. The concentrated solution underwent a purification process by dialysis using a 1 kDa MWCO membrane, and the procedure lasted for 2 days. The dialysis process involved replacing the water every 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, coarse particles were removed via centrifugation, and the resulting solution, containing brown MCDs, was subjected to dialysis. The MCDs were then stored at 4 °C for subsequent characterization and analysis.

The quantum yields of MCDs have been measured with the help of anthracene (Φ = 0.27).
Φ
s a m p l e
=
Φ
s t d

I

/

A

/
A

(
η

/
η
s t d
)
2

The symbol Φstd represents the quantum yield of the reference sample, which in this case is anthracene. Isample/Istd denotes the intensity ratio of the sample (MCDs) to the standard (Anthracene). Astd/Asample represents the absorbance ratio of the standard and sample. Lastly, ηsample/ηstd denotes the ratio of refractive indices between the sample and the standard.

In this study, a new method for measuring hypochlorous acid (HOCl) in water at room temperature was developed using a fluorometric assay. To perform the assay, MCDs were dispersed in deionized water at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL, and different concentrations of HOCl (ranging from 0 to 15 μM) were added to the solution. MCDs react quickly with HOCl, shown by fluorescence signal loss in 1 min, indicating their efficiency for timely HOCl detection. The fluorescence emission spectra were then acquired at room temperature, using excitation at 380 nm. To validate the specificity of HOCl detection, a range of other anions (namely, Br−, Cys, GSH, Hcy, HCO3 −, HS−, HNO, H2O2, OH, HSO4 −, O2 .−, ONOO−, NO2−, ClO−, I− and SO3 2−) were examined, in a manner analogous to the HOCl measurement.

We assessed the potential cytotoxicity of multi-colored luminescent carbon dots (MCDs) towards RAW 264.7 cells in this study using the MTT (methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium) assay. RAW 264.7 cells were cultured with MCDs at concentrations ranging from 0 to 1 mg/mL in a 96-well cell-culture plate. Subsequent to this, the cellular proliferation was cultured in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C for a duration of 24 h. For the MTT assay, 10 mL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was administered to each well, and the plates were further incubated for 4 h at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere. After removal of the MTT solution, the resultant golden formazan crystals were dissolved in a solution of 200 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and 25 mL of Sorensen's glycine buffer (0.1 M glycine and 0.1 M NaCl). The optical density of each well was subsequently assessed at 570 nm using a Multiskan GO microplate scanner. Cell viability was then calculated utilizing the provided formula:
(1)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
( M e a n
o f
a b s o r b a n c e

a b s o r b a n c e
v a l u e
o f
c o n t r o l
g r o u p )

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and confocal luminescence imaging of MCDs in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: The RAW 264.7 cell line was cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% (v/v) streptomycin and 100 μg/mL fetal bovine serum and maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2. To stimulate endogenous ROS production, the cells were treated with 1 μg/mL phorbol myristate acetate (PMA) at 37 °C for 2 h. The culture medium was then removed, and the treated cells were washed with 1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to eliminate residual medium. Subsequently, cellular fluorescence images were acquired using a Leica TCS SP5 X AOBS confocal microscope (Germany) equipped with a 63-oil-immersion objective lens. The excitation wavelength was set at 410 nm, and the emission wavelength was recorded at 440 ± 10 nm.

In this study, the synthesis, structural, and morphological characterization of mushroom carbon dots (MCDs) prepared by autoclaving at 200 °C. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis revealed that the MCDs had an amorphous structure, with an average size range of 1–3.5 nm in an aqueous solution (Fig. 1a and b) [5]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra showed a broad peak at 2θ = 210, indicating the presence of graphitic carbon domains (Fig. 1c) [8]. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to investigate the functional groups on the surface of the MCDs. The results revealed broad lines at 3318 cm−1 and 2921 cm−1, which were mainly attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and C–H groups, respectively [30]. In addition, the bands observed at 1615 and 1512 cm−1 were notably correlated with the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively [32]. The stretching vibrations of C–N and C–O–C were found to be associated with the 1443 and 1320 cm−1 bands, while the band at 1032 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of C–O (Fig. 1d) [36,37]. The presence of these surface functional groups plays a crucial role in imparting water solubility and stability to the MCDs, thereby providing compelling evidence of their successful preparation.

To explore the optical properties of the MCDs, absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were employed. The absorption spectra of the MCDs revealed a peak around 320 nm, which corresponds to the π-π∗ transition linked with aromatic CC bonds. Upon excitation at a wavelength (λ ex) of 380 nm, the MCDs demonstrated strong fluorescence emission at a maximum wavelength (λ max) of 441 nm. The λ max and intensities of the fluorescence spectra were further investigated by varying the λ ex from 380 to 560 nm, as displayed in Figure S1 [11].

The effect of various anions such as Br−, Cys, GSH, Hcy, HCO3 −, HS−, HNO, H2O2, OH, HSO4 −, O2.−, ONOO−, NO2 −, I − and SO3 2− on the absorption signal of the MCDs was found to be negligible. However, upon the addition of HOCl, the characteristic orange color of the MCDs faded and became colorless (Fig. 2 a), indicating a response to the presence of HOCl. To ascertain the selectivity of the MCDs, their fluorescence response was assessed in the presence of different ROS and other anions. Notably, HOCl ions were observed to cause a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the MCDs compared to other anions (Fig. 2b). This result can be attributed to the higher affinity of the MCDs towards HOCl, owing to the presence of surface functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen [57]. The luminescent MCDs reacted rapidly with HOCl, resulting in a reduction of the fluorescence intensity within a minute (see SI Figure S2). These findings underscore the potential of the MCDs for rapid detection of HOCl.

The antioxidant capabilities of MCDs were evaluated using a colorimetric assay based on the discoloration of the oxidized state of DPPH (violet) to its reduced form (yellow). As displayed in Fig. 5 a, the impact of different concentrations of MCD on DPPH scavenging activity was investigated. Both BHT and MCDs were found to considerably inhibit the DPPH radical, confirming the antioxidant potential of MCDs. MCDs' capacity to scavenge free radicals increases with concentration, and the remarkable discovery was that MCDs had a higher antioxidant activity of 89% at 100 g/mL. Conversely, conventional BHT, at the same concentration, showed 79.64% inhibition. One of the key factors in cancer development, mutagenesis, and cytotoxicity is the hydroxyl radical (•OH). The concentration-dependent method of MCDs scavenging the •OH radical is presented in Fig. 5b. MCDs scavenging potential was substantial at all tested doses, with a maximum inhibition of 83%, while BHT used as a positive control, successfully scavenged hydrogen radicals with 78% activity at 100 g/mL. The results indicate that MCDs have distinctive optical and electronic properties, including carboxylic acids and hydroxyl groups that make them excellent electron donors and antioxidants. MCDs can scavenge free radicals and quench singlet oxygen, making them useful in various applications, such as antioxidants.

The primary aim of this study was to broaden the scope of MCDs utilization in exploring endogenous HOCl in biological systems. To achieve this goal, the MCDs probe was utilized to assess the endogenous production of HOCl by RAW 264.7 cells, a well-established macrophage cell line frequently implicated in inflammation and injury response [62]. Furthermore, a viability assay on RAW 264.7 cells was conducted to assess the cytotoxicity of the MCDs in the presence of PMA. The findings indicated that MCDs were relatively non-toxic to cells, with a cellular viability of over 87.3% after one day of incubation (Fig. 6). The study demonstrates that MCDs have excellent membrane permeability and low cytotoxicity against living cells, making them an ideal luminous probe for live cell imaging applications. These cells were used for live bioimaging of HOCl in biological systems. To enhance the endogenous production of HOCl in the RAW 264.7 cell line, the cells were treated with a concentration of 25 ng/mL of PMA (as depicted in Fig. 7). After incubating the cells for two hours, 0.2 mg/mL of MCDs were introduced and allowed to react with the cells' HOCl formation for another 30 min. Fluorescence suppression, as seen with confocal imaging, indicates that PMA stimulates HOCl generation in RAW 264.7 cells. These findings support further investigation into the potential of MCDs as a sensitive method for identifying intracellular HOCl in cells.

In summary, an environmentally sustainable methodology has been established for synthesizing MCDs with a prompt response to HOCl. In deionized water, the MCDs exhibit remarkable specificity towards HOCl compared to other reactive oxygen species and anions. Through the use of a luminescent titration technique, MCDs exhibited exceptional sensitivity in detecting even minute HOCl concentrations, as low as 3.38 nM, that falls within the physiological range of HOCl in vivo. This innovative approach was extended to facilitate the real-time visualization of endogenous HOCl in RAW 264.7 cells, while the MTT assay demonstrated that MCDs had no toxic effects on these cells. RAW 264.7 cells were also employed as a model to investigate the presence of endogenous HOCl in imaging studies. For bioimaging purposes, PMA was employed to induce endogenous HOCl formation in biological systems. In addition to providing a novel natural source for MCD synthesis, this research demonstrates the diverse potential of MCDs in bioimaging and antioxidation.

Parthiban Venkatesan: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing Original draft preparation. Rajakumar Govindasamy, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing- Original draft. Govindasami Periyasami, Formal analysis, Writing- Original draft. Mostafizur Rahaman, Formal analysis, Writing- Original draft. Saravanan Pandiaraj, Formal analysis, Writing- Original draft. Muthu Thiruvengadam, Formal analysis, Writing- Original draft. Natesan Thirumalaivasan, Writing - Review & Editing, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Supervision. Shu-Pao Wu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSPD2023R674), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this research work. This study was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (Taiwan) under a grant (MOST 111-2113-M-A49-001).
